text: I really thought it was going to be a great game, and the first hours were great, but then you start realizing there's no depth at all to this game, both figuratively and literally. It really feels like it was intended to be a story game, and then someone decided they wanted it to be a survival game, so it's a bit of both. But the survival elements are superficial, and the story is extremely predictable and dull. The one positive thing is that the underwater atmosphere is amazing... But even then biomes are copy paste, the map is very small, etc It's still an ok game if you go in not expecting too much. To me it feels like a bit less than half of a game. Like I reached the literal end of it, thinking I was finally reaching mid-late game, still expecting upgrades and exciting things that never came

text: I found the game grindy and very frustrating. It felt like working rather than playing a game. I rage-completed it but I didn't enjoy it. I understand why people love the game. I was lured into it as I was searching for something that could scratch my Outer Wilds itch. I even saw many people who don't like crafting and survival games say the loved it. So I thought it was a no-brainer for me (despite not liking crafting and survival). Well, I was frustrated throughout the game. It really marred the early game experience which was really cool with some amazing early dives. But then comes the resource gathering. The never ending resource gathering. And the inventory management. I swear I spent more time managing inventory than I did playing the game. The game forces so many grindy mechanics it's downright insane to me. And it gets really, really bad in the third act, where for no proper reason we have to find some random items from the world after finally making it to a really difficult to get to place. But no, let's do it again. And again. Because grinding is a lifestyle. This could have been less frustrating but the interface is also quite rough around the edges. Want to craft something with materials you have in your base? Not so fast! First go through your cupboards to put all those materials in your inventory, THEN craft (staying in base the whole time). Want to build something? Let me remember is it the upgrade module, the fabricator, or the modifier? Time to go to each to figure it out. Oh and where do I put that upgrade module I just built? Especially for one ship it was quite unclear. Also the base building: I found it quite frustrating that it's basically impossible to know in advance WHY you need each module and what they will do for you. And at least for me, my first base refused to connect to simple things like the Scanner Room and I had no idea what to do. It's definitely made for gamers who are used to this type of games. What about the story? Honestly it's a bit meh and it doesn't make much sense when you think too much about it. By the middle of the game I was treating it like work to be done. I just rage-completed the damn thing.
label: analysis
notes: Difficult to separate the emotional part and the user experience from the claims they made against the game. But under the `Feature requests, mechanic complaints, and claims wrapped in a personal story`, this falls under analysis as it still makes claims against the game, despite being wrapped in emotional statements.

text: Gather, craft, ad nauseam.
label: analysis

text: No idea how this game got the sales and positive reviews it has. I'm rarely baffled, but this is ridiculous. You get 45 seconds of air in your oxygen tank. That alone is ruining the experience for me. You're effectively stuck in a very short range around your "pod", which is so comically small you wouldn't wish it as a prison cell to your worst enemy. There's nothing to see. I was expecting to be wowed by magnificent sea creatures, high quality graphics, and a satisfying crafting system. Instead there's just a few small weird "alien" fish and a bunch of rocks; not even a smashing animation, you just click them, and you have this single "fabricator" which magically and instantly converts your stuff into other stuff. It's so incredibly rudimentary, yet it's not a $5 game. They charge $30 for it. It's a 2014 indie game, but it feels like a 2006 one. Hell, the PS2 had countless better titles than whatever this is. Comically, my 2080 Ti also can't even reach 165fps on 1440p, it hovers around 110-130. But the game looks like it should run on a toaster. I'm not new to open world survival that doesn't hold your hand. I've played Don't Starve for 1000+ hours. But that game was actually fun, full of character, well-balanced, elaborate and enjoyable to play. It didn't punish you for exploring by choking you after 45 seconds, so like that's a start. Maybe Subnautica could start there. Jesus man.
label: analysis

text: + atmosphere + complex story + base building is cool - monsters are just a nuisance. no boss fights. no combat. - no grinding. no real reason to build a base - you need to "stumble" upon clues to progress. You can't build or grind anything to move on - if you miss a key detail, you can waste 10 hours mindlessly wandering around without a goal. You will not progress. The game will not tell you why. You just need to re-explore the same places and find the little piece of metal behind a certain desk in a certain cave at a certain location in the 4km / 4km world. - ~100-150 PDAs (pages) of text. Some are important for progression. Some are not. You need to read all of them to know which is which If you're lucky and explore "correctly" you'll enjoy the game. If you're unlucky, easily miss details or struggle to find games fun if you don't have a specific goal to work towards, then Subnautica is NOT the game for you.
label: analysis

text: HIGHLY RECOMMENDED I originally played Subnautica on the Xbox One and I HATED the game, it didn't feel good at all and I couldn't bring myself to play much of it. Now I have beaten the game on PC, and I ended up really liking it. To get the negatives out of the way, I really hate the way Subnautica hides essential upgrades. Whilst some wrecks are marked for you via the story and contain useful equipment, most are not and you have to just guess which wrecks you've explored and which you haven't, which can pretty easily lead to missed equipment, upgrades and buildings which some are REQUIRED to beat the game. I wish there was either a visual indicator that you had explored something, or maybe a voice clip to let you know that you had found everything. Also, some enemies are bugged up the ass. Some enemies later in the game (spoilers ig) can just insta-kill you and I found out that it's just a bug which can happen, which is pretty bad considering it's sometimes unavoidable to take hits if you find yourself in a bad situation. Also the ai voice is bugged, and told me that I was entering the void AFTER I HAD ALREADY FALLEN IN WITH THE PRAWN, LEAVING IT FOR DEAD. Aside from those negatives, I really enjoyed Subnautica. Sure, I needed a guide for some things, but like Terraria, I believe that the game becomes far more fun when you're working towards goals rather than flailing around aimlessly in the endless ocean. The story is pretty good and helps to provide something to always work towards, and the general gameplay is just fun. I loved making bases and watching them come together, and whilst the grinding became pretty annoying, it always gave me something to do. I personally don't think Subnautica is a masterpiece or anything, but I do think it's a really good game, that is worth experiencing if you think you'll be interested. Stick with it, and I hope you'll find the addiction the same way I did.
label: analysis

text: Unbelievable how this exploration game punishes you for exploring. "Congrats on building that sweet submarine -- now watch it get wrecked by one of the two dozen sea dragons we placed all around the world." Re-building it is not impossible but it does waste your time and make exploring with it again nerve-wrecking. Not to mention the potential for it to kill you and drop your collected loot if you are deep enough and not still in the 100-300m range that I have been struggling to escape from. Progression switches between inconsistent and frustrating. Unlocked battery-powered equipment like a flashlight? Get ready to fill your very limited inventory spaces with 0% batteries or just not use it yet cause you need more tech. Looking for Copper Ore? Just break the Limestone Outcroppings. Obviously. "Here's some hints on where to find Lifepod 7... what do you mean you haven't already stumbled into the Crag Fields and have deployed two dozen Beacons all over the explored world to help orientate yourself?! Go watch some more YT tutorials instead of exploring in this exploration game!!" This last bit is particularly frustrating because the Radio mechanism that drip feeds you objectives works in a strict sequence of events completed, so I don't know where to go next without first discovering where these directions the game gave me are wrong. Dang, it's so dark in here -- better use my flashlight decked out with recharged batteries and WHOOPS, the light attracts monsters that killed me for being so stupid as to explore in this exploration game. How silly of me! I better build some reinforcements to expand my base and WHOOPS, I built it on the section of this multi-purpose room where connectors are supposed to be built for some reason, cannot be deconstructed anymore! Awesome, lemme jank this up as penance for not watching enough YT tutorials. I bet this game is amazing to play THE SECOND TIME AROUND, as the first time includes hours circling around the same dumb ideas and misguided intuitions. It's gotten to the point where I don't even care about being careful with my submarine anymore and just started recklessly driving into random directions hoping something helps me understand where I am supposed to be heading to next or why I care about any of this at all. Quit after another sea dragon destroys it, re-load from the beginning and pick a new direction. Also, why does ESC exit from fabricator interfaces but TAB exits from the inventory ones? Why can't I drink/eat items from storage directly instead of first moving them to my inventory, TAB, then TAB again to THEN be able to drink/eat? Why can't I repair the Seamoth while it's docked in the Moonpool? So help me god, if I find out these issues are all addressed with mods, I'm going to scream.
label: analysis
notes: Extensive game design critiques with examples from experience

text: I am writing this review as a long-term fan of the series and an M2 Pro (16GB RAM) user. While Subnautica is a masterpiece in terms of game design and atmosphere, the current state of the native macOS port is fundamentally broken and practically unplayable, even on high-end Apple Silicon hardware. Despite having capable hardware, I am experiencing persistent FPS drops and severe performance instability, often fluctuating significantly even on the lowest graphical presets. Here are the specific technical grievances. The graphics settings menu is largely unresponsive. For instance, adjusting 'Water Quality' or texture settings often results in no visible changes to the rendering pipeline. It appears the game fails to hook into the current Metal API properly, leaving the engine stuck in a degraded state. The native macOS build is severely outdated and lacks optimization for modern macOS environments. It seems to rely heavily on legacy frameworks that struggle to interface with Apple Silicon, leading to inefficient resource allocation and frequent frame pacing issues. Even when scaling down settings, the frame rate remains unstable, hovering between 50-60 FPS with constant, jarring micro-stuttering. This suggests a significant lack of proper culling or efficient draw call management, which is unacceptable for a system that should easily handle this title. For a game of this caliber, the lack of support and optimization for macOS is disheartening. As a developer-in-training, I recognize that maintaining a native port is resource-intensive; however, in its current state, the experience is subpar compared to what this machine is capable of. I strongly recommend that users on Apple Silicon avoid the native macOS version and look into running the Windows version via compatibility layers (like CrossOver/GPTK), as the native port is currently neglected. I hope the developers eventually revisit the macOS build and provide the necessary updates to align with modern graphics APIs.
label: analysis

text: TLDR; I can no longer recommend it.. just got the prawn suit.. and its 'default' forrm is USELESS to explore the depths (after leaving the cyclops), unless it has a ramp down to those depths.. because you cant jump up the shear walls that I've come across! you need 'upgrades' and I have no idea where to get the resource (nickel, without 'cheating').. I spent too long (died) trying to get the thing out off the trench, back to the cyclops.. but even without that issue, this game is fundamentally flawed.. the 'day/night' cycle is FAR too quick and for no reason! there are no 'quests' (you get optional objectives) that are time based to require quick day/night cycles.. so the devs intentionally made the cycle quick to WASTE YOUR TIME! the default air tank has 45 seconds IRL.. which equates to about an hour of in game time (assuming around 20 hours for the day time component! several hours for night!).. so while thats 'ok'.. its just about everything else that is wrong.. if it takes you 45 seconds to walk a few hundred metres! that took you 1 in game hour! which is completely moronic.. these devs either didnt think it trhough or ignored glaringly obvious stupid issues... in order to achieve their objective of small amounts of time for the air tank.. quick degradation to food.. and your hunger needing to be satisfied ALL THE TIME! along with MANY other mechanisms that are impacted by this day/night cycle being so short IRL.. and this game has plenty of other issues! mostly around 'how to do' stuff! and the mining/construction side of things, which is ludicrous.. you pick up this 'concentrated' form of material..and can either make a tiny amount of something (copper wire) or a HUGE platform! what are these ppl thinking?!! the 'abstraction' of making stuff is a complete distraction! while the game does have its 'fun' moments, its ridiculous that you have to constantly use external sources to find out how to do something.. its one thing to find a 'blueprint' on how to make something which shows the materials you need, but no mention of whats required to make it (construction 'gun' vs fabricator vs advanced construction module).. and then there is the 'how do you recharge it'! not even a hint of it in the DB.. honestly these devs are moronic! at least give information in the DB! in any 'technologically advanced' civ.. they would have ALL the required information at hand. this game is an exploration game.. it is NOT a 'horror survival' game..there is no real combat required (you can just 'run away'). and there is no 'horror'.. a few monsters dont equate to horror! and the only issue in regards to 'survival' is due to the pathetic mechanics these devs implemented! but the biggest issue, as per any 'survival'... not enough storage (they give you these ridiculously small containers).. and a ridiculously small amount of resources scattered FAR AND WIDE! I found silver to be a pain in the butt to find.. and then when you get the scanner it only can scan a small distance! what?! they've never heard of a MOBILE metal deterctor!?! everything is geared to WASTE YOUR TIME (some moronic manager probably said "you MUST ensure they take 10s of hours to do 'X'")! instead of having a mandate to ensure the player has fun! instead they demand you constantly feed 'yourself'.. where you spend half your time chasing down food/water.. its annoying.. sure there is a game option to not have to worry about it.. but its the dev mindeet thats the issue! I dont mind getting food.. I have 2 meals a day and exercise.. why does this 'survivor' need to be fed hourly!?! we need to be able to adjust the timeframe..along with resource nodes that deliver more than 1 of something! (or at least a metal detector you can carry around!), because moving your base to another location so you can get more material is just stupid. anyway, after I got sick and tired of these ridiculously small containers, I decided to leave some stuff lying about on the ground.. tested for a while, nothing disappeared! so I started leaving lots of stuff on the ground.. what disappeared.. my good stuff.. rubies, gold, silver, etc!! really annoyed! no mans sky is a MUCH better implementation of actual exploring, survival and building (even under water!).. AND ts actually fun to play!

text: Subnautica is an amazing game that truly captures the amazement of the deep sea and creates a fun a varied experience. Near the beginning, subnautica starts as an exploration and story game (an element which does remain throughout). Later in the game (I have not completed it yet), it adds a interesting building mechanic and you start to need to scan a lot more for fragments to find blueprints. As the game progresses you also get the ability to go deeper into unique terrain. One thing that really impressed me was how well they have done the first person and how seamless the interactions with items like the fabricator are (I haven't played loads of big games like this so it might be a standard thing). Overall, subnautica has been one of the most enjoyable games I have played for a while and I can't wait to complete it and play the next 2 games.
label: analysis

text: This game has all you look for when trying to find a horror game. Is it a horror game? Nope, it is a survival game that will gladly make you need to change your pants eventually. This game feels like it would literally run on ANYTHING that has barely the minimum required RAM, as a matter of fact minimum requirements feel to be more like a recommendation than an actual rule. The environments are BEAUTIFUL(even on the lowest settings), the flora and fauna makes you adore the game. Then you find the leviathans and rethink your life choices. Is it an enjoyable game? yes, it is very enjoyable, and it will push you to overcome EVERY SINGLE one of the fears of what's outside of the safe shallows eventually. And that by itself is super rewarding(IMO), since every single minute, not only you're easing into overcoming your fears, but learning from it and getting used to it while also learning about the story(which btw, is REALLY good) Is it a punishing game? Kind of, the game itself is not really super hard, but the mental toll it'll cause you if you are distracted or you already have thalassophobia it will ABSOLUTELY make you regret some decisions. Is this game hard to beat? If you don't like collecting resources, or if you are someone who likes more visual novels than real survival games or just simply... don't have patience?... yes, otherwise, this game is absolutely beatable, and easy if you are clever enough. Overall? This game's a 10/10 with amazing visuals, impressive optimizations, great compatibility, and even better history. Absolutely Worth buying, and even more worth to put hours into, as the game is really rewarding, especially after finishing it, and will surely make you want to play it over and over again regardless of how much fear it causes you.
label: analysis

text: Really wanted to like this game, but I don't think it's for me. The visuals are pretty good, but I got tired of the crafting pretty quickly (as in most games). It just feels like busy work whilst trying to go through the story. Randomly some radio messages pop up telling you where to go, where there's just some PDA and nothing much progresses. It feels like nothing is happening at all. Honestly most of this I was reasonably ok with, even though I was getting a bit bored. I hated it when I decided to quit and instead of saying "are you sure" it said "last save 40 minutes ago". 40 minutes it the amount of time I usually have for gaming these days. So yeah I didn't read it right and just said "ok". Welp there went 40 minutes of progress. How do you not have an auto-save function before quitting the game? That should be standard practice these days. Gave up after that.
label: experience

text: I have tried again and again to like this game. At first it was a coworker who recommended it to me and really wanted me to play it. Then it was my girlfriend, who loves this game. Yet, every time I tried, every time I pushed myself to give this game a go, it just felt pointless, like I was wasting my time. The game didn't feel to me as though it was giving me any incentive to... play. You explore underwater ruins to find blueprint for all kinds of stuff, but there isn't anything to work towards. Sure, you can build submarines and stuff eventually, but what's it all for? This game unfortunately lacks any sort of incentive to play it at all. The visuals, though dated, are stunning, and the sound design is brilliant. It's a really beautiful set piece, but that's just what it is, a set piece. It feels like there isn't a game to play but just... again, a set piece for a story, perhaps.
label: experience

text: Was enjoying the game, then while exploring my vehicle (seamoth) teleported under the ground. It had storage and in that were all the green cubes and purple tablets of the game.
